description: Psychological dark patterns: covert manipulation tactics used to undermine a target's perception, autonomy, or decision-making. Paraphrased from psychological literature; NOT a verbatim transcript of any copyrighted work.

entries.description: Persistently denying or distorting someone's perception of events to make them doubt their own memory, sanity, or judgement.
entries.source: Psychology: Dorpat (1994); Stern (2007) 'Gaslighting: The Art of Psychological Manipulation'. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Overwhelming a person with excessive affection, flattery, and attention as a precursor to control or exploitation.
keywords: I've never met anyone like you
you're the only one who understands me
entries.source: Psychology: Lancer (2014); Singer (1995) on cult recruitment patterns. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Escalating a sequence of requests from small and innocuous to large and harmful, exploiting the target's tendency for consistency.
entries.source: Freedman & Fraser (1966), J. Personality & Social Psychology. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Creating artificial urgency or false scarcity to pressure someone into making a hasty decision they might not otherwise make.
entries.source: Cialdini (1984) Influence: paraphrased principle of scarcity. Not verbatim.

entries.description: Systematically cutting a person off from friends, family, or support networks to increase their dependence on the manipulator.
entries.source: Domestic abuse literature: Evan Stark (2007) Coercive Control. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender — a defensive manipulation pattern where the perpetrator denies wrongdoing, attacks the accuser, and claims to be the real victim.
keywords: look what you made me do
entries.source: Freyd (1997) DARVO acronym in Journal of Trauma & Dissociation. Paraphrased.

entries.description: Introducing a third party — real or fabricated — to make the target feel jealous, insecure, or competitive, thus increasing control over them.
entries.source: Personality disorder literature; Ross Rosenberg (2013). Paraphrased.
